costs: Administrative burden of producing alternatives assessments for each proposed closure
Possible delay in addressing genuinely unviable schools
Training costs for tankerületi staff to conduct assessments
content.equity_impact: Provides villages and disadvantaged communities, who currently lack formal standing, a documented voice in closure decisions; does not by itself address segregation risk from church-school alternatives, but increases transparency around closures affecting Roma-concentrated schools (F-live-education_finance-06).
expected_benefits: Reduces arbitrary or purely cost-driven closures by forcing consideration of community and educational effects
Creates a transparent, appealable record replacing informal protest-driven reversals
Aligns Hungary with established European closure-prevention practice
content.goal: Prevent avoidable closures of viable village primary schools by requiring formal, documented consideration of alternatives before any closure decision, replicating the Scotland/Wales procedural safeguard within Hungary's centralized governance structure.

implementation_steps.action: Draft and enact amendment to the Public Education Act establishing a presumption-against-closure duty and mandatory alternatives-assessment procedure for tankerületi center closure decisions
implementation_steps.timeline: within 12 months, timed to the 2026 governance restructuring

implementation_steps.action: Develop standardized alternatives-assessment templates (educational benefit, community impact, transport impact) and publish decisions with reasoning
Formal right to submit alternatives and receive written response within statutory timeframe; right of appeal to a designated review body

mechanisms: Codifying a presumption-against-closure duty analogous to Scotland's model forces documented consideration of alternatives, educational benefits, community effects, and travel arrangements before closure (F-live-international_comparison-02).
Currently closures are decided via administrative 'temporary closure'/'suspension' actions without a dedicated statutory procedure specific to demographic decline (F-live-legal_and_governance-04).

Existing reversals of closure decisions happen only through informal political-administrative intervention after protest, not codified appeal (F-live-legal_and_governance-08, F-live-political_feasibility-07).

The 2026 governance reform already plans to restore some principal/municipal input while retaining centralized authority, providing a legislative vehicle for adding procedural safeguards (F-live-hungarian_education_system-07, F-live-legal_and_governance-09).
risks: Procedural safeguard may become a formality if not paired with genuine authority to halt closure, given centralized ownership of the maintenance function (F-live-international_comparison-04)
Risk of inconsistent application in absence of independent oversight body
Political resistance from central authorities protecting administrative discretion
content.title: Legally codified presumption-against-closure with mandatory alternatives assessment
